decision: SSG because homepage marketing shell is static and optimized for global caching.
SSG because this is static informational brand content with no per-request personalization.
account route uses dynamic user session context and is treated as SSR by default runtime behavior; reverse if route is redesigned to fully static public profile content.
SSG because this route is static affiliate-intake guidance with manual follow-up and no verified public program terms.
blog route is cache-friendly semi-dynamic content and should use cache components/runtime cache behavior; reverse if per-user personalization becomes mandatory.
ISR because article content is semi-dynamic and should refresh on a timed cadence.
SSG because the careers route renders a static informational shell and remains noindex while not part of the canonical public SEO graph.

SSG because this route is a fixed sales-collateral catalogue whose content is updated editorially rather than per-request. It remains noindex,follow because it functions as a premium catalogue sheet, not a canonical search landing page.

checkout route is transaction-sensitive and remains request-time rendered under Next.js 16 Cache Components defaults; explicit `dynamic` segment config is removed because it is incompatible when cacheComponents is enabled. Reverse only if checkout is redesigned to static-safe behavior.
SSG because this educational overview is static informational content.
ISR because condition detail pages are content-driven and update periodically.
SSG because contact details and form shell are static route content.
SSG because policy content is static and versioned by deploy.
SSG because FAQ content is curated static educational material.
SSG because this compliance/informational page is static content.
SSG because this legacy assurance route is a static, non-indexable trust summary that points buyers to the canonical purchase-policy pages.
SSG because help center structure is static support content.
SSG because this product information page is static marketing content.
SSG because /learning is a noindex routing hub that should route users into real protocol, research, store, rental, and support pages without client-only gating.
SSG because the locator is a noindex utility page backed by repo-controlled partner records.
SSR because authentication entry experiences are user-state aware.
SSG because this route is static product/editorial content.
nexus landing stays server-rendered as a static, public, noindex operator overview so the route can explain the workspace clearly while deeper modules stay interactive in client leaves.
nexus academy stays server-routed as a temporary public preview while the build-in-progress training workspace remains noindex and interactive behavior stays in a client leaf.
nexus clients stays server-routed as a temporary public preview while the build-in-progress CRM surface remains noindex and interactive behavior stays in a client leaf.
nexus docs stays server-routed as a temporary public preview while the build-in-progress document workspace remains noindex and interactive behavior stays in a client leaf.
nexus fleet stays server-routed as a temporary public preview while the build-in-progress operations dashboard remains noindex and interactive behavior stays in a client leaf.
nexus studio stays server-routed as a temporary public preview while the build-in-progress creation tool remains noindex and interactive behavior stays in a client leaf.
nexus supplies stays server-routed as a temporary public preview while the build-in-progress reordering workspace remains noindex and interactive behavior stays in a client leaf.
nexus team stays server-routed as a temporary public preview while the build-in-progress staff dashboard remains noindex and interactive behavior stays in a client leaf.
SSR because onboarding flow can depend on user/session context.
SSG because partner program overview is static business information.
ISR because press assets and mentions can update on a periodic cadence.
SSG because privacy policy is static legal content tied to deploy revisions.
product route is catalog-style semi-dynamic content and should rely on cache components/runtime caching; reverse if product data becomes per-user personalized.
SSG alias route that permanently consolidates legacy hydrogen model paths onto the new dedicated static PDPs.
SSG because this product page is a fixed, indexable hydrogen PDP with stable product data.

ISR because protocol index content is semi-dynamic and periodically updated.
ISR because protocol detail content is editorial and refreshed periodically.
ISR because rental catalog and pricing context is semi-dynamic.
SSR because checkout is personalized and must render per-request state.

research route is an indexable, content-heavy evidence hub and should render the answer block, review context, and study links on the server; keep runtime caching behavior and avoid client-side filters so the page stays crawlable and faster to parse.
SSG because returns policy content is static legal-operational guidance.
SSR because rewards visibility can depend on authenticated user state.
SSG because this is a lightweight internal search shell that reads query params and should not be indexed.
SSG because shipping policy and timelines are static informational content.
store route is semi-dynamic catalog content and should use cache components/runtime cache behavior; reverse if inventory/pricing moves to strict per-request rendering.
redirect because /support is a duplicate trust alias and should resolve to the canonical help center.
SSG because terms are static legal content versioned by deploy.
SSG because this is a private noindex lab route with a static server shell and an isolated client-side interaction leaf.
SSG because warranty policy content is static legal-commercial guidance.

wellness planner route stays a static, noindex shell with a server-rendered orientation block and an interactive client island for the recommendation flow. Cache components/runtime cache behavior is preferred unless server-personalized data is introduced.
SSR because wishlist data is user-specific and session-dependent.
